blocks:
Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِأَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْفِيلِ (١)
Have you not regarded how your Lord dealt with the army of the elephants? (1)
أَلَمْ يَجْعَلْ كَيْدَهُمْ فِى تَضْلِيلٍ (٢)
Did He not make their stratagems go awry, (2)
وَأَرْسَلَ عَلَيْهِمْ طَيْرًا أَبَابِيلَ (٣)
and send against them flocks of birds (3)
تَرْمِيهِم بِحِجَارَةٍ مِّن سِجِّيلٍ (٤)
pelting them with stones of shale, (4)
فَجَعَلَهُمْ كَعَصْفٍ مَّأْكُولٍۭ (٥)
thus making them like chewed-up straw? (5)

(Commentary)

The chapter contains an allusion to the story of the People of the Elephant (أصحاب الفيل) when they intended to attack Makkah to destroy the Honored Kaaba (الكعبة المعظمة). Allah destroyed them by sending swarms of birds (طير أبابيل) that pelted them with stones of baked clay (سجيل), rendering them like chewed-up straw (عصف مأكول). This is among the manifest signs (آيات) of Allah that are beyond concealment; the people of the pre-Islamic era (الجاهليون) used it as an epoch for their calendar and mentioned it in their poetry. The Surah is Meccan.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِأَصْحابِ الْفِيلِ — "Have you not regarded how your Lord dealt with the companions of the elephant?" (105:1) The intended meaning of "seeing" (الرؤية) here is knowledge (العلم) that is as clear as sensory perception. The interrogative is for the purpose of disavowal (إنكاري), meaning: "Do you not know how your Lord dealt with the People of the Elephant?" This event occurred in the same year in which the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was born.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ يَجْعَلْ كَيْدَهُمْ فِي تَضْلِيلٍ — "Did He not make their stratagem go awry," (105:2) Their "stratagem" (كيدهم) refers to their evil intent toward Makkah and their desire to destroy the Sacred House (البيت الحرام). The terms tadlil (تضليل) and idlal (إضلال) both mean "leading astray" or "causing to go awry." Making their stratagem go awry means making their efforts stray so that they could not reach their intended goal. They set out to destroy the Kaaba, but their journey ended in their own destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَرْسَلَ عَلَيْهِمْ طَيْراً أَبابِيلَ — "and send against them flocks of birds," (105:3) The term ababīl (أبابيل)—as has been said—refers to groups in succession, flock after flock. The meaning is that Allah sent against the People of the Elephant separate groups of birds. This verse and the one following it serve as an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير) for His saying: "Did He not make their stratagem go awry."

His saying, the Exalted: تَرْمِيهِمْ بِحِجارَةٍ مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ — "pelting them with stones of shale," (105:4) That is, the flocks of birds pelted the People of the Elephant with stones of baked clay (سجيل). The meaning of sijjīl (سجيل) has already been discussed in the commentary on the stories of the people of Lot.

His saying, the Exalted: فَجَعَلَهُمْ كَعَصْفٍ مَأْكُولٍ — "making them like chewed straw?" (105:5) The term asf (العصف) refers to the leaves of crops. "Chewed straw" (العصف المأكول) refers to crop leaves whose grain has been eaten, or the husks of grain whose kernel has been consumed. The meaning is that after the stones of baked clay fell upon them, they became bodies without souls, or that the heat of the stones incinerated their insides. It has also been said that it refers to crop leaves afflicted by decay (الأكال), which is when worms eat and spoil them. The verse has been explained in some other ways that do not accord with Quranic etiquette.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘: The narrators are in consensus that the king of Yemen who intended to demolish the Kaaba was Abraha ibn al-Sabbah al-Ashram. It is said his agnomen (كنية) was Abu Yaksum. It is narrated from al-Waqidi that he was the grandfather of the Negus (النجاشي) who lived during the time of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

The author then recounts the story of his seizure of power in Yemen until he says: Then he built a "kaaba" in Yemen and placed within it domes of gold, commanding the people of his kingdom to perform pilgrimage to it, thereby rivaling the Sacred House. A man from the tribe of Banu Kinanah traveled there, looked at it, and then sat in it—meaning to relieve himself. When Abraha entered and found that filth there, he said: "Who dared do this to me? By my Christianity, I shall surely demolish that House [in Makkah] so that no pilgrim shall ever visit it again!" He called for the elephant and announced the expedition to his people and those of Yemen who followed him; most of those who followed him were from the tribes of ‘Akk, al-Ash‘arun, and Khath‘am.

He said: Then he set out, and while on his way, he sent a man from Banu Sulaym to call the people to pilgrimage to the house he had built. He was met by a man from the Hums (الحمس) of Banu Kinanah who killed him, which increased Abraha's rage, and he hastened his march.

He requested a guide from the people of al-Ta’if, so they sent with him a man from Hudhayl called Nufayl. He led them until they reached al-Mughammas, where they encamped—it is six miles from Makkah. They sent their advance parties into Makkah, while the Quraysh fled in separate groups (عباديد) to the mountain peaks, saying: "We have no power to fight these people." No one remained in Makkah except ‘Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim, who stayed to attend to his duty of providing water (سقاية), and Shaybah ibn ‘Uthman ibn ‘Abd al-Dar, who stayed to guard the House (حجابة). ‘Abd al-Muttalib took hold of the two doorposts of the Kaaba and said:

O Allah, a man protects his own dwelling, so protect Your precincts. Let not their cross and their might overcome Your might. If they are to enter the Sacred City, then it is a matter You have ordained.

Then the advance parties of Abraha seized some livestock belonging to Quraysh, including two hundred camels belonging to ‘Abd al-Muttalib ibn Hashim. When news of this reached him, he went out to meet the people. Abraha’s chamberlain was a man from al-Ash‘arun who knew ‘Abd al-Muttalib, so he sought permission for him to see the king, saying: "O King, the master of Quraysh has come to you—he who feeds the humans in the valley and the wild beasts in the mountains." He said: "Let him enter."

‘Abd al-Muttalib was a man of imposing stature and great beauty. When Abu Yaksum saw him, he felt too much respect for him to make him sit below him, yet he disliked sitting him with him on his throne. So he descended from his throne, sat on the ground, and sat ‘Abd al-Muttalib beside him. Then he asked: "What is your need?" He replied: "My need is for the two hundred camels of mine that your advance party seized." Abu Yaksum said: "By Allah, I saw you and you impressed me, but then you spoke and I lost interest in you." He asked: "And why, O King?" He replied: "Because I have come to the house that is your glory, your protection from the Arabs, your excellence among people, your honor over them, and your religion which you worship—I have come to break it, yet you ask me about your camels and do not petition me regarding your House."

‘Abd al-Muttalib said to him: "O King, I am speaking to you about my property. As for this House, it has a Lord Who will protect it; I have nothing to do with that." This startled Abu Yaksum, and he ordered that ‘Abd al-Muttalib’s camels be returned to him. ‘Abd al-Muttalib returned, and that night was a grim one for them; the stars seemed so close they were almost speaking to them, and their souls sensed the impending punishment.

Until he said: At sunrise, the birds appeared over them carrying stones and began pelting them. Each bird had one stone in its beak and two stones in its claws. When a bird finished pelting, it would leave and another would appear. No stone fell upon a person's belly but that it pierced through it, nor upon a bone but that it weakened and punctured it. Abu Yaksum turned back in retreat, having been struck by some of the stones. As he reached each land, a limb of his would fall off, until he reached Yemen and nothing remained of him but a wreck. When he arrived, his chest burst open, his belly split, and he perished. Not a single person from the tribes of al-Ash‘arun or Khath‘am was harmed. (The tradition continues).

I say: There are significant differences in the narrations regarding the specific details of the story. Whoever wishes to examine them should consult the detailed works of biography (سير) and history.
